text: The surgeon creates a window in the pars flaccida, a thin membrane located in the lesser omentum, to facilitate access to the hepatic hilum.
A disposable plastic trocar is inserted into the left upper abdomen, and umbilical tape is threaded through the window in the pars flaccida and then through the foramen of Winslow.
The umbilical tape, previously introduced through the window in the pars flaccida and the foramen of Winslow, is shown with a marked center point.
The surgeon threads the ends of the umbilical tape through the disposable plastic trocar positioned in the left upper abdomen, completing the setup for the laparoscopic Pringle maneuver.
The surgeon uses a disposable plastic trocar with soft edges to avoid traumatizing the porta hepatis during the procedure.
During the right hepatectomy, there is visible bleeding from a portal branch.
The surgeon deploys the Pringle maneuver by advancing the trocar over the umbilical tape and externally tightening a clamp to occlude the hepatic inflow.
The surgical field shows effective hemostasis following the Pringle maneuver, allowing the hepatectomy resection to proceed without active bleeding.
A trocar and grasper are introduced through a port positioned high in the right upper abdomen, providing access for further manipulation during the hepatectomy procedure.
The surgeon navigates through the foramen of Winslow, reversing direction during the right hepatectomy procedure.
The surgeon grabs the umbilical tape, which is used in the Pringle maneuver to control bleeding during the right hepatectomy.
The surgeon positions the umbilical tape in the appropriate location and grasps its ends, threading them through a plastic disposable trocar introduced in the surgical field.
The surgeon places a Rommel tourniquet laparoscopically, utilizing the umbilical tape through the plastic disposable trocar to control blood flow during the hepatectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully deploys an umbilical tape, positioning it within the surgical field using laparoscopic instruments.
The surgeon brings the ends of the umbilical tape out through a lower abdominal incision and loads them onto a red rubber catheter.
A clamp is placed externally on the red rubber catheter, which can be advanced similarly to a Rommel tourniquet in open surgery, indicating readiness for vascular control.
The surgeon positions the red rubber catheter in the surgical field, preparing it for potential use as a Rommel tourniquet during the hepatectomy procedure.
